Open to opportunities

Richard Gamarra

Data Analyst — Python · SQL · Tableau · Excel

My last project uncovered that over 90% of expert responses to farmer questions never mentioned the crop or animal the farmer actually asked about — a finding from analysing 5.87 million questions for Producers Direct, a UK charity supporting over one million smallholder farmers in East Africa. That is the kind of work I do: take large, messy datasets and surface findings that change how an organisation thinks. I also built a multilingual pipeline using Meta’s NLLB-200 to translate 5,000 questions from three African local languages into English — unlocking data that had never been analysed before.

01 — About

About me

I am a data analyst with hands-on experience cleaning, analysing, and communicating insights from real-world datasets — ranging from 21,000 rows to 7.25 GB. My most recent project, a volunteer role with Producers Direct (a UK charity supporting over one million smallholder farmers), found that over 90% of expert responses on their platform never answered the farmer’s actual question. That finding came from analysing 5.87 million records — and it changed how the organisation understood its data quality problem.

I hold a Bachelor’s degree in Economics from George Mason University (2023), which gave me a strong foundation in analytical thinking, research methods, and communicating findings to non-technical audiences. I apply that same structured approach to every data project: define the question clearly, clean the data rigorously, document every decision, and deliver findings that are easy to act on.

My technical toolkit includes Python (pandas, NumPy, Plotly), SQL (MySQL, DuckDB), Excel, and Tableau. I also have practical NLP experience: I built a translation pipeline using Meta’s NLLB-200 model to convert 5,000 farmer questions from three African languages into English — unlocking a dataset that had never been analysed before.

I am actively looking for full-time, contract, or freelance data analyst roles where analysis directly informs business decisions — not just reporting for its own sake.

DataKind · Prep ChallengeNLPTranslation

Making Farmer Data Usable: Translating 5,000 Questions from Three African Languages

Problem: 3.24 million of Producers Direct’s farmer questions were written in Swahili, Luganda, and Runyankore/Rukiga — languages the data team could not read, so this portion of the dataset had never been analysed at all. What I did: Built an automated translation pipeline using Meta’s NLLB-200 model, a transformer specifically designed for low-resource African languages. Validated the output quality and structured the translated data for downstream use. Outcome: Delivered a farmer language glossary and a structured, AI-training-ready dataset — the first time these languages had ever been processed in the platform’s history.

DataKind · Challenge 4SQLEDA

What Do 5.8 Million Farmer Questions Actually Tell Us?

Key finding: over 90% of expert responses to crop and livestock questions never actually mentioned the crop or animal the farmer asked about. When WeFarm closed in 2022, Producers Direct inherited 7.6 million unanalysed questions. I cleaned the raw data down to 5.87 million unique questions using DuckDB, classified all 148 topic labels across four countries and four years, and delivered a stakeholder report with charts and written recommendations — identifying exactly where the platform’s response quality was failing the farmers it was supposed to serve.

Cleaning the GameZone Retail Orders Dataset in Excel

Problem: A 21,864-row retail orders dataset had inconsistent date formats, missing values, duplicate entries, and invalid records that made any downstream reporting unreliable. What I did: Applied a structured data cleaning process in Excel — fixing issues that could be resolved automatically, flagging issues that required business decisions, and adding derived columns such as order age and fulfilment status. Outcome: Delivered a clean, analysis-ready file alongside a documented quality log — so any analyst picking up the data can see exactly what was changed and why.

IndependentSQLTableau

Retail Customer Segmentation Using RFM Analysis

Problem: A retail business was treating all customers the same — running identical campaigns regardless of whether someone had bought once last year or ten times last month. What I did: Cleaned 541K raw transaction records in MySQL, removed invalid data, and used SQL window functions to score each customer on how recently they bought, how often, and how much they spent. This produced 8 distinct customer segments. Outcome: Built a Tableau dashboard and written strategy recommendations showing which customers to prioritise for retention, which to target for win-back, and which were likely lost — giving marketing a clear starting point for campaign planning.

DataKind · Prep Challenge NLPTranslationText Mining

Making Farmer Data Usable:
Translating 5,000 Questions from Three African Languages

Producers Direct operates digital platforms that help over one million smallholder farmers in Kenya, Uganda, Tanzania and Ghana access agricultural advice. Their dataset, originally built by the WeFarm SMS platform, contains 7.6 million farmer questions. Of those, approximately 3.24 million were written in local languages — Swahili, Luganda and Runyankore/Rukiga — that the data team could not process. Those questions existed in the database but were unavailable for any analysis, topic modelling or AI model training.

This project addressed that gap. A sample of 5,000 non-English questions was translated into English using Meta's NLLB-200 model, which was specifically designed for low-resource African languages where standard tools perform poorly. Quality checks were run to validate the output. Two structured datasets were then delivered to Producers Direct's AI team: a farmer language glossary capturing how farmers actually describe agricultural problems, and a keyword-tagged version of all 5,000 questions ready for supervised model training.

Note: this project ran in parallel with the Challenge 4 question analysis. Challenge 4 worked with the English-language questions already in the dataset. This project prepared the non-English questions for future use. The two workstreams used different parts of the same dataset and were not dependent on each other.

ClientProducers Direct (via DataKind UK)
CompletedDecember 2025
Dataset size5,000 questions in 3 languages
Tools usedPython, HuggingFace, DuckDB, Pandas
View NotebookGitHub Repo
SummaryMetricsDataOutputsMethodologyInsightsCaveats
KEY
Executive Summary: All 5,000 non-English farmer questions were successfully translated into English, with 4,918 (98.4%) passing quality checks and ready for immediate use. The analysis revealed that farmers consistently use informal, everyday language rather than technical agricultural terms — a critical insight for any AI model trained on this data. Two datasets were delivered: a farmer language glossary organised by category, and a keyword-tagged question set that provides a training-ready signal for each question. These outputs save Producers Direct's AI team from manually annotating thousands of rows by hand, and ensure that future models are trained to understand how farmers actually communicate.
Key Performance Indicators
5,000
Non-English farmer questions translated into English
98.4%
Passed automated quality checks and are ready to use
3
Source languages translated: Swahili, Luganda, Runyankore/Rukiga
2
Structured datasets delivered to Producers Direct's AI team
Why standard translation tools were not appropriate for this task: Swahili, Luganda and Runyankore/Rukiga are all classified as "low-resource" languages by AI standards, meaning they have limited training data available compared to widely-spoken languages like English, Spanish or French. Commercial tools such as Google Translate perform poorly on these languages and frequently produce incorrect or garbled output. The NLLB-200 model from Meta was selected because it was purpose-built for exactly this challenge — trained on 200 languages specifically including Swahili, Luganda and Runyankore/Rukiga. Using the right tool for the language profile of the data was a prerequisite for producing output that the AI team could actually trust.
How the data broke down by language
Questions by source language
The 5,000 questions were drawn from three source languages. The breakdown reflects the geographic spread of WeFarm users across the region.
  • Swahili made up 57% of the sample (2,877 questions), reflecting its widespread use across Kenya and Tanzania.
  • Runyankore/Rukiga accounted for 39% (1,927 questions), representing farmers primarily in Uganda.
  • Luganda made up the remaining 4% (196 questions), also predominantly from Uganda.
Translation quality results
Three types of quality checks were run on the translated output.
  • Completeness check: Every one of the 5,000 questions produced a non-empty English output. There were zero missing translations.
  • Length-ratio check: Any translation more than four times longer than the source text was flagged as a likely repetition error. This caught 82 rows (1.6%), which were separated out before building the glossary.
  • Manual spot-check: A sample of translations from each language was reviewed by hand to confirm that key farming terms like maize, chickens, fertilizer and pest were being translated correctly and preserved in the output.
What the translations actually look like

These are four real questions taken directly from the dataset, shown alongside their NLLB-200 translations. Reading them is important context for understanding the rest of this project. Farmers are not asking theoretical questions — they are asking about specific, urgent problems happening right now on their farm. A rabbit with a skin disease. A calf that just died. Fleas on their chickens. This directness and specificity shaped every decision I made when building the glossary and keyword dataset.

Swahili → English
"sungura wangu wako na upele niwape ndawa gani?"
— NLLB-200 translation —
"What medication should I give my rabbit that has mange?"
Runyankore / Rukiga → English
"E ENTE YANJE EZAIRE ENYENA YASHOBERA."
— NLLB-200 translation —
"My cow gave birth but the calf died after five minutes."
Swahili → English
"dawa ya viroboto kwa kuku"
— NLLB-200 translation —
"What is the medicine for fleas in chickens?"
Swahili → English
"napaswa kula nyama ya ngombe ambaye alikufa usiku?"
— NLLB-200 translation —
"Should I eat the meat of a cow that died at night?"
Output 1 — The farmer language glossary
Why a farmer language glossary was needed: Farmers do not use technical agricultural vocabulary. They describe problems in the same everyday language they use to talk about anything else. A farmer experiencing nutrient deficiency in their crops will not say "nutrient deficiency" — they will say "why are my plants turning yellow." A farmer whose chickens have avian respiratory disease will not use that term — they will say "my chickens are coughing." If a future AI model is trained only on formal agricultural text sourced from textbooks, research papers or official guidance, it will fail to recognise what farmers are actually asking, because the vocabulary is completely different. The glossary solves this problem by documenting the informal language farmers actually use and mapping it to the correct agricultural categories. It acts as a bridge between how farmers speak and how formal models are trained.
Number of glossary terms per category
After scanning all 5,000 translated questions for high-frequency agricultural vocabulary, terms were grouped into four categories. The breakdown tells us what farmers care about most.
  • Livestock had the most terms (8), with chicken, cow, goat and hen appearing most frequently. Animal health is clearly the top concern across all three languages.
  • Crops came second (7 terms), led by maize and beans, which farmers frequently pair with questions about pests, fertilizer and yield.
  • Soil, weather and pests had 6 terms. These often appear alongside other categories rather than as standalone topics.
  • Inputs and treatments had 4 terms, including fertilizer, spray and medicine, which appear across both crop and livestock questions.
Sample terms from the glossary
The highlighted terms below appeared most frequently across the 5,000 translated questions. Larger, teal-coloured terms were the most common in the dataset.
Livestock — most common category
chickencowgoatchickshenpigrabbit
Crops
maizebeanspotatosunflowertomatokale
Inputs & treatments
fertilizersprayseedmedicine
Environment
soilpestdiseaseweatheryield
Output 2 — The keyword-tagged dataset
What the keyword-tagged dataset does and why it matters: For each of the 5,000 translated questions, I searched the English text for any terms from the glossary and recorded which terms were found and which categories they belong to. The result is a structured dataset where every question has a label — for example, "livestock + inputs" if a farmer asked about medicine for their chickens, or "crops only" if they asked about maize growth. This gives Producers Direct's AI team a training-ready signal for every question without having to manually read and label thousands of rows. Manual labelling at this scale would take weeks and introduce human inconsistency. The automated keyword-tagging approach does it in seconds and produces a consistent, reproducible result that can be rerun as new questions are added to the dataset.
How many questions mention each combination of categories
This chart shows the proportion of the 5,000 tagged questions that fall into each combination of categories. The most important finding is how often questions cross category boundaries.
  • 28% of questions were about livestock only, making this the single largest individual category in the tagged dataset.
  • 24% of questions mentioned both livestock and crops within the same question — for example, a farmer asking whether to feed a certain type of maize to their chickens.
  • 19% of questions were about crops only, and 15% spanned three or more categories at once.
  • The implication for model design is clear: a classifier that assigns one label per question will misclassify nearly half the dataset. Any model trained on this data must support multi-label classification.
Methodology

The work followed five steps in sequence. Each step produced an output that fed directly into the next.

  • Step 1 — Selected the 5,000 questions. Used DuckDB to query the full WeFarm dataset, filtering for rows where the detected language was Swahili, Luganda or Runyankore/Rukiga and the question content field was not empty. The 5,000 questions were selected as a representative working sample of the 3.24 million non-English questions in the full dataset.
  • Step 2 — Translated all 5,000 questions using NLLB-200. Loaded Meta's facebook/nllb-200-distilled-600M model via HuggingFace Transformers and ran it on GPU in Google Colab at approximately 1.72 questions per second. Set max output length to 400 tokens to ensure longer farming questions were translated in full rather than truncated.
  • Step 3 — Validated translation quality using three checks. First, confirmed that every one of the 5,000 questions produced a non-empty output. Second, flagged any translation whose English output was more than four times longer than the source text, as this reliably indicates machine repetition errors. This identified 82 rows (1.6%) which were separated out. Third, manually reviewed a sample of translations from each language to confirm that key farming terms like maize, chickens, fertilizer and pest were being preserved correctly in the English output.
  • Step 4 — Built the farmer language glossary. Counted word frequencies across all translated questions and focused on agricultural vocabulary. Organised the most frequent terms into six categories: livestock, crops, inputs and treatments, soil, weather, and pests and disease. For each term, recorded its frequency count and pulled up to three real example questions where it appeared. Saved as farmer_glossary_enhanced.csv.
  • Step 5 — Built the keyword-tagged dataset. For each of the 5,000 translated questions, searched the English text for any terms from the glossary, stored the matched terms in a keywords_found column and their categories in a keyword_categories column. Saved as farmer_keyword_dataset_with_categories.csv. This dataset gives Producers Direct's AI team a structured, labelled signal for every question without requiring any manual annotation.
Insights Deep Dive

Five insights emerged from translating and tagging the 5,000 questions. Each has a direct implication for how Producers Direct should train and design future AI models.

  • Farmers describe agricultural problems in plain, everyday language rather than technical terms. A farmer experiencing nitrogen deficiency says "why are my beans not growing." A farmer whose chickens have Newcastle disease says "my chickens are falling over." This gap between how farmers communicate and how formal agricultural models are trained is the core problem this glossary was built to solve. Any model trained only on technical text will fail to match these questions to relevant answers.
  • Livestock is the most frequently mentioned subject across all three languages, with chicken and cow appearing more often than any crop term. This pattern held consistently in Swahili, Luganda and Runyankore/Rukiga, suggesting that animal health is a top priority for farmers regardless of which country or language they are from. Future model training should reflect this weighting.
  • 24% of questions reference more than one category within a single question. Farmers regularly ask about chickens eating maize, or about applying fertilizer to sick plants. A classification model that assigns one label per question will misclassify nearly one in four questions. The keyword dataset was structured specifically to support multi-label approaches to account for this.
  • Treatment and dosage questions appear across both crop and livestock categories, not just one. Terms like fertilizer, spray and medicine appear in questions about both plants and animals. This means content about how much to apply and when to treat would serve farmers across categories simultaneously, making it a high-leverage content investment for Producers Direct.
  • Weather appears as a background factor rather than a primary topic. Weather terms rarely appear as the main subject of a question but co-occur frequently with pest, soil and yield questions. This suggests weather is an important underlying variable that future models should factor in even when it is not the explicit focus of the question.
Assumptions and Caveats
Limitations to be aware of: This work used a sample of 5,000 questions from a full non-English dataset of 3.24 million questions, so the word frequencies in the glossary may shift when applied at full scale. NLLB-200 occasionally produces awkward or slightly unnatural phrasing in Runyankore/Rukiga, which is the least-resourced of the three languages and had the fewest training examples available to the model. The keyword list captures the most common agricultural terms but will miss less frequent, regional or colloquial expressions that did not appear often enough in the sample to be included. Any AI model trained on this data should be re-evaluated when new questions are added to the dataset, as farmer vocabulary may evolve over time.

DataKind · Challenge 4 SQLEDAData Visualisation

What Do 5.8 Million Farmer Questions
Actually Tell Us?

ClientProducers Direct (via DataKind UK)
CompletedDecember 2025
Dataset size7.25GB — 20.3M rows, deduplicated to 5.87M
Countries coveredKenya, Uganda, Tanzania, Ghana
View NotebookGitHub Repo
BackgroundData structureExecutive summaryDashboardInsightsRecommendationsMethodology
Project Background

Producers Direct is a UK charity that operates digital platforms helping over one million smallholder farmers in Kenya, Uganda, Tanzania and Ghana access agricultural advice. In 2022 they took stewardship of the full WeFarm data archive — seven years of farmer-to-farmer conversations from an SMS platform. The dataset contains 7.6 million questions and 17 million responses that had never been analysed at scale.

This project thoroughly analyses and synthesises that data to uncover insights that will improve how Producers Direct serves its farmers — identifying what content to prioritise, where response quality is falling short, and how demand shifts by country and season.

Insights and recommendations are provided on the following key areas:

  • Topic Concentration: Understanding which crops and livestock dominate farmer demand, and how focused content investment can reach the largest number of farmers.
  • Country-Level Variation: An evaluation of how question types differ across Kenya, Uganda, Tanzania and Ghana, and why a single regional strategy will not serve all four markets equally.
  • Seasonal Trends: Analysis of how question volumes shift across the agricultural calendar, and when new content needs to be published to reach farmers at the right moment.
  • Response Quality: An assessment of whether the responses farmers receive are specific enough to actually help them, measured by whether responses mention the crop or animal the farmer asked about.
Data Structure & Initial Checks

The WeFarm dataset was provided as a single 7.25GB CSV file containing 20.3 million rows. The structure below shows the key fields available for analysis. Prior to beginning the analysis, a range of quality checks were conducted to confirm data completeness, identify duplicates, and understand the shape of the data before any counts or aggregations were run.

wefarm_questions
question_idString
question_sentTimestamp
question_contentString
topic_labelString
language_detectedString
country_codeString
platformString
wefarm_responses
response_idString
question_idString
response_contentString
response_sentTimestamp
responder_countryString
topic_classification (derived)
topic_labelString
categoryString
category_detailString
Initial checks confirmed: Zero missing question content fields. 17.4% of rows had no topic label (set aside as "Unknown"). Only 28.89% of the 20.3 million rows were unique questions — the rest were duplicates created by the relational structure, where each question was repeated once per response received. This made deduplication the essential first step before any analysis could begin.
Executive Summary
Overview of Findings

After deduplication, 5.87 million unique farmer questions were analysed across four countries and four years. Question demand is highly concentrated — the top 15 topics account for 73% of all questions, meaning a focused content strategy for those topics would reach the vast majority of farmers on the platform.

Demand follows a clear seasonal pattern, peaking at up to 200,000 questions per month during East Africa's two main planting seasons. The most significant finding is a response quality gap: over 90% of responses to crop-specific and livestock-specific questions do not mention the specific crop or animal the farmer asked about. Farmers are asking specific, targeted questions and receiving generic answers. The following sections explore each of these findings in detail and close with six concrete recommendations for Producers Direct.

Key Performance Indicators
Total unique questions
5.87M
after deduplication from 20.3M raw rows
Topic labels classified
148
across 4 categories: crop, livestock, general, unknown
Top 15 topics share
73.4%
of all classified questions in just 15 topics
Repeat question rate
19%
of English questions are exact word-for-word duplicates
DataKit Dashboard
DataKit Farmer Question Intelligence Dashboard
Producers Direct / DataKind UK · 5.87M unique questions · 4 countries · 2018-2021
01Top 15 topics account for 73.4% of classified demand, showing strong content concentration.
02Country mix varies sharply, so one regional content strategy would under-serve some markets.
03Topic-match rates are low: only 14.5% for crop questions and 11.0% for livestock questions.
Question type breakdown
Farmers mostly ask specific crop and livestock questions, not broad farming theory.
Most asked-about topics
The highest-volume topics show where content investment can reach the most farmers.
Question mix by country
Question types vary by country, especially Ghana and Kenya.
Response topic-match rate
Specific farmer questions often receive generic responses.
Seasonal demand by question type
Question volume peaks around planting seasons, so content should be published before demand spikes.
Data preparation: why deduplication was the essential first step. The raw dataset contained 20.3 million rows, but only 28.89% of those rows represented unique questions. The WeFarm database stored each question once for every response it received, so a question that attracted ten responses appeared ten times in the file. Analysing the raw rows would have inflated every count by approximately 3.5 times, producing misleading results across every dimension of the analysis. A question_level view was created in DuckDB to collapse the 20.3 million rows down to 5.87 million unique questions before any counting or analysis began. Every figure in this analysis is based on that deduplicated dataset.
Insights Deep Dive

Five insights emerge from this analysis. Each one leads directly to the recommendations in the next section.

  • Farmer demand is highly concentrated, with 73.4% of all questions falling within just 15 topics. Maize alone has 535,569 questions. Chicken and cattle together add another 855,920. Building deep, specific guides for the top five topics would address the most common questions for the majority of farmers on the platform.
  • The question mix varies significantly by country, meaning a single regional content strategy will be a poor fit for at least two of the four countries. Ghana is 70.5% crop-specific while Kenya is only 41.9% crop-specific. A content plan built for one country will systematically under-serve another.
  • Question volume follows East Africa's agricultural calendar, peaking sharply during the two main planting seasons each year. Demand reaches 180,000 to 200,000 questions per month during March to May and October to December. Content needs to be published before these peaks, not during or after them.
  • The most significant finding is the response quality gap: over 90% of responses to crop and livestock questions never mention the specific crop or animal the farmer asked about. The topic-match rate is 14.5% for crop questions and 11.0% for livestock questions. Farmers are asking specific questions and receiving generic responses.
  • 19% of English questions are exact word-for-word repeats of questions already in the database, suggesting farmers are not finding existing answers. Improving search and answer discovery would reduce repeat submissions without requiring any new content to be created.
Insight 1 — What types of questions do farmers ask?
Question type breakdown — 4.1M classified questions
Of the 4.1 million questions that had topic labels, three distinct types emerged. The breakdown tells us what kind of help farmers are primarily seeking.
  • 51.1% of questions were crop-specific, meaning they asked about a named crop such as maize, tomato or beans.
  • 38.3% were livestock-specific, meaning they asked about a named animal such as chicken, cattle or goat.
  • 10.6% were general farming questions that were not tied to a specific crop or animal.
  • Only 0.04% could not be classified at all, confirming the classification system covered almost the entire dataset.
  • The overall picture is clear: farmers are not coming to the platform for broad agricultural theory. They want specific, targeted help with a named crop or animal they are dealing with right now.
Note on the 4.1M figure: 17.4% of questions in the original WeFarm dataset had no topic label assigned. These were set aside as "Unknown" before analysis. The 4.1M classified questions represent the 82.6% of the dataset that did have topic labels.
The 8 most-asked-about topics
The top 15 topics account for 73.4% of all classified questions. The concentration of demand in a small number of topics has a direct practical implication for content strategy.
  • Maize is the single most asked-about topic with 535,569 unique questions, nearly 100,000 more than the second-place topic.
  • Chicken (430,894) and cattle (425,026) are second and third, reflecting the importance of poultry and livestock across the region.
  • Tomato (326,011) is the fourth most asked-about topic, showing strong interest in high-value vegetable crops.
  • Maize, chicken and cattle together account for 1.39 million questions — nearly one quarter of the entire classified dataset.
  • The implication is that building deep, high-quality guides for the top five topics alone would address the most common questions for the majority of farmers on the platform.
Insight 2 — Does the question mix vary by country?
Why this finding matters for content strategy: If all four countries asked the same types of questions in the same proportions, one content strategy would serve all of them. The data shows this is not the case. The variation between countries is substantial enough that a strategy built for Ghana would be a poor fit for Kenya, and vice versa. Any content investment decision should be broken down by country rather than treated as a single regional plan.
Question type split by country
The question type split varies significantly across all four countries. The chart below shows these differences clearly.
  • Ghana has the highest proportion of crop-specific questions at 70.5%, meaning more than two in three questions from Ghanaian farmers are about a named crop.
  • Tanzania is second at 63.8% crop-specific and has the lowest proportion of general questions of any country at just 5.2%.
  • Uganda sits in the middle at 54.2% crop-specific, with a relatively even balance between crops and livestock.
  • Kenya has the lowest proportion of crop-specific questions at 41.9% and the highest proportion of general farming questions at 12.8% — more than double Tanzania's figure.
  • A single content strategy built for all four countries at once would be poorly matched to the actual needs of farmers in at least two of them.
This variation likely reflects genuine differences in farming practice by country. Ghana's agricultural economy is heavily crop-based, while Kenya has more diverse livestock activity and a higher share of general farming questions.
Insight 3 — Does demand follow a seasonal pattern?
Monthly question volumes by type, 2018–2021
Monthly question volumes from January 2018 to July 2021, broken down by question type. Two patterns stand out immediately.
  • Question volume shows a clear seasonal pattern, with sharp increases during East Africa's two main planting seasons: March to May (long rains) and October to December (short rains).
  • All three question types — crop-specific, livestock-specific and general — rise and fall together. This is a platform-wide seasonal effect, not a pattern driven by any one crop or topic.
  • At peak, combined monthly question volume reaches approximately 180,000 to 200,000 questions across all types. Crop-specific questions account for the largest share of each seasonal spike.
  • Content published after a seasonal peak has already missed the window when farmers are actively planning. New guides should be ready four to six weeks before the March and October peaks begin.
Insight 4 — Are the responses farmers receive specific enough to be useful?
How response quality was measured: A "topic-matched" response was defined as one that directly mentions the specific crop or animal named in the farmer's question. For example, a response to a maize question counts as topic-matched only if it mentions maize. This is a conservative measure — a response can be genuinely useful without being topic-matched — but a very low rate is a reliable signal that responses are generic rather than tailored. This metric was chosen because it is objective, reproducible and directly tests whether the platform is connecting specific questions with specific answers. The results were striking.
What general questions are actually asking about
Among the 446,000 general farming questions, the topics break down as follows. The dominant theme has a clear content implication.
  • Planting and timing is the largest category at 29.8%, covering questions about when to plant, how to plant and what conditions are needed. Because this applies across all crops, a single well-written cross-crop planting guide would address nearly 30% of all general demand.
  • Disease questions account for 4.3% of general questions, covering plant and animal health issues that are not tied to a specific named species.
  • Pests account for 3.2%, soil questions for 2.4%, fertilizer for 1.0% and weather for 0.8%.
  • The remaining 58.6% fall into a mixed category covering questions that span multiple themes or do not fit neatly into a single label.
What % of questions received a topic-matched response
The chart shows what percentage of questions in each category received at least one response that mentioned the specific crop or animal asked about.
  • General farming questions had the highest match rate at 35.5%. This is expected because broad questions are easier to match with broad answers.
  • Crop-specific questions had a topic-match rate of only 14.5%. This means that if a farmer asks a question about their maize, fewer than 1 in 7 responses they receive will mention maize at all.
  • Livestock questions were even lower at 11.0%. For every ten farmers asking a specific question about their cattle or chickens, nine received responses that never named the animal they asked about.
  • Together, these figures show that over 90% of responses to the most common question types on the platform are generic rather than specific. Farmers are asking targeted questions and receiving answers that were not written with their situation in mind.
Recommendations

Six recommendations follow directly from the data. Each recommendation is tied to a specific finding and quantified where possible.

  • Prioritise content investment in the top five topics: maize, chicken, cattle, tomato and poultry. These five topics alone account for over 1.9 million questions — a third of the entire classified dataset. Building comprehensive, specific guides for these topics would address the most common questions for the majority of farmers using the platform. This is the highest-return starting point for any new content effort.
  • Produce a cross-crop planting guide as a standalone resource. 29.8% of all general farming questions are about planting timing and technique. Because this topic applies across all crops rather than a single named species, one well-written planting guide would address nearly 30% of all general demand with a single investment. This is unusually high leverage compared to topic-specific guides.
  • Develop country-specific content strategies rather than a single regional plan. Ghana is 70.5% crop-focused. Kenya is 41.9% crop-focused with 12.8% general questions. The gap between these two countries is too large for one plan to serve both effectively. Content priorities should be set at the country level.
  • Introduce a response quality metric and set a target for improvement. The current topic-match rate is 14.5% for crop questions and 11.0% for livestock questions. A simple keyword-match score would make this gap visible, trackable and improvable over time. Increasing this rate is the most direct way to improve the quality of help farmers receive from the platform.
  • Publish new content four to six weeks before the March and October seasonal peaks. Question volumes reach 180,000 to 200,000 per month at peak. Content published during or after a peak misses the window when farmers are actively planning and making decisions. Advance publication ensures guides are available exactly when demand is highest.
  • Invest in search and answer discovery to reduce the 19% repeat question rate. Nearly one in five English questions is an exact repeat of a question already in the database. This suggests farmers are submitting questions rather than searching for answers that already exist. Better search and recommendation features would improve the farmer experience without requiring new content to be produced.
Assumptions and Caveats
Caveats & Assumptions: Response quality was measured by keyword matching rather than semantic understanding. This means a response that uses a synonym or describes the crop without naming it would not be counted as topic-matched, even if it was genuinely helpful. The 14.5% and 11% figures should therefore be read as a lower bound rather than the definitive truth. The 19% repetition figure is based on exact text matching of English-language questions only — repetition in Swahili, Luganda or Runyankore/Rukiga was not measured and could be higher or lower. Some topic labels in the original WeFarm data were ambiguous — for example, the label "plant" could reasonably be classified as either crop-specific or general — and in a small number of cases the category assignment required a judgement call rather than a definitive rule.
Methodology

The analysis followed five steps. Each step built on the previous one, and the order mattered.

1
Load and quality-check the data. The 7.25GB CSV was loaded into DuckDB. Initial checks confirmed zero missing question content, that 17.4% of rows had no topic label (these became the "Unknown" category), and critically, that only 28.89% of the 20.3 million rows were unique questions. The rest were duplicates created by the relational database structure. This made deduplication the essential first step before any counting or analysis.
2
Deduplicate to question level. A question_level view was created that collapsed 20.3 million rows down to 5.87 million unique questions using GROUP BY question_id with MIN(question_sent) to keep the earliest timestamp for each question. Every number in this analysis is based on this deduplicated view.
3
Classify all 148 topic labels. Every distinct topic label was manually reviewed and assigned to one of four categories: Crop-Specific, Livestock-Specific, General, or Unknown. This classification was saved as a topic_classification table and joined back to the question data.
4
Run the analysis across four dimensions. Four separate analyses were run on the deduplicated, classified dataset: question type distribution, topic concentration, monthly seasonal trends from 2018–2021, and country-level variation across Kenya, Uganda, Tanzania and Ghana. A fifth analysis measured response quality by checking what proportion of questions received at least one topic-matched response.
5
Visualise and document findings. Charts were built for each dimension: stacked area for seasonal trends, 100% stacked bar for country comparison, horizontal bars for topic rankings, donut charts for question type distributions, and a bar chart for response specificity. All findings were written up with full SQL query logs attached.
© 2026 Richard Gamarra · Data Analyst← Back to portfolio

IndependentExcelData Cleaning

Cleaning the GameZone
Retail Orders Dataset

Tool Used
Microsoft Excel
Record Count
21,864 rows
Dataset
gamezone_orders_data
Framework
CLEAN
View Excel File Documentation
BackgroundExecutive summaryIssues logExcel functionsWhat the data suggestsCaveats
Project Background

GameZone is a simulated e-commerce retailer selling gaming products. The raw orders dataset contained 21,864 records with a range of data quality issues typical of real-world business data: inconsistent date formats, missing values, duplicate order IDs, invalid entries, and incorrect regional mappings.

This project focused on transforming that messy dataset into an analysis-ready format using Excel, following the CLEAN framework — Conceptualise, Locate, Evaluate, Augment, Note — to ensure every decision was documented and defensible.

The cleaning work covered the following key areas:

  • Date standardisation — Fixing inconsistent timestamp formats and extracting date parts for time-based grouping.
  • Product name normalisation — Standardising inconsistent naming for the same product across 61 rows.
  • Missing value handling — Resolving blank fields in marketing channel, account creation method, and regional data.
  • Duplicate detection — Identifying and documenting 145 duplicate order IDs.
  • Data augmentation — Adding derived columns (time-to-ship, cleaned timestamps, regional mapping) to enable downstream analysis.
Executive Summary

The dataset contained 10 identified data quality issues across 8 columns. The most significant was 2,000 rows where the ship date occurred before the purchase date — 9.1% of all records — which could not be corrected without stakeholder input and was flagged for transparency. Seven of the ten issues were fully resolved. The cleaned dataset is now ready for downstream sales analysis, regional reporting, and trend identification.

21,864
Total rows in the raw dataset
10
Data quality issues identified across 8 columns
7/10
Issues resolved — 3 required stakeholder input
2,000
Rows with ship date before purchase date (9.1%)
Issues Log

Every data quality issue identified across the dataset is documented below, including whether it was solvable and how it was resolved.

Column(s) Issue Description Row Count Solvable Resolution
purchase_ts Inconsistent datetime formatting, invalid timestamps 10 Yes Used Excel DATE functions to reformat: =DATE(YEAR(C2), MONTH(C2), DAY(C2))
purchase_ts 1 missing value 1 No Left as-is (low magnitude — 0.005% of records)
product_name Inconsistent naming for the same product 61 Yes Standardised names using an IF formula: =IF(I2="27inches 4k gaming monitor", "27in 4K gaming monitor", I2)
usd_price $0 or missing price values 34 No Left as-is; would require stakeholder clarification to resolve
marketing_channel Missing values 83 Yes Replaced blanks with "unknown": =IF(N2="", "unknown", N2)
account_creation_method Missing values 83 Yes Replaced blanks with "unknown": =IF(P2="", "unknown", P2)
country_code Missing values 27 No Left as-is; insufficient info to impute
region (lookup table) Missing / inconsistent regional values 9 Yes Filled in with correct values using VLOOKUP: =VLOOKUP(R2, region_cleaned!A:C, 3, FALSE)
order_id Duplicate records 145 Yes Documented; low volume, left for review — not deleted
ship_ts < purchase_ts Ship date occurred before purchase date 2,000 No Flagged for transparency using =E2>=D2; not deleted — 9.1% of records
Methodology & Key Excel Functions
1
Date standardisation. =DATE(YEAR(C2), MONTH(C2), DAY(C2)) stripped malformed timestamps into clean date values.
2
Product name normalisation. =IF(I2="27inches 4k gaming monitor", "27in 4K gaming monitor", I2) standardised 61 inconsistent rows.
3
Missing value imputation. =IF(N2="", "unknown", N2) replaced blanks in marketing channel and account creation method.
4
Regional mapping. =VLOOKUP(R2, region_cleaned!A:C, 3, FALSE) corrected missing region values using a lookup table.
5
Ship date validation. =E2>=D2 flagged 2,000 rows where ship date preceded purchase date — kept for transparency, not deleted.
6
Time-to-ship derivation. =E2-D2 created a TIME_TO_SHIP column enabling downstream fulfilment analysis.
What the data suggests

Cleaning a dataset surfaces more than just formatting errors. The patterns in the issues log itself tell a story about how the business operates. Three observations follow directly from what was found.

Observation 1 — The fulfilment process likely has a data recording problem, not a time-travel problem

  • 2,000 rows (9.1% of all records) show a ship date that precedes the purchase date. At that volume, this is almost certainly a systematic recording error rather than random data entry mistakes.
  • In practice, this pattern most commonly occurs when an order management system logs the ship timestamp at the point of warehouse despatch preparation rather than at confirmed purchase. If the warehouse processes orders in batches overnight and the purchase timestamp is applied the following morning, the ship date will consistently appear to precede the purchase date.
  • For a marketing or operations analyst using this dataset, this has a direct consequence: any analysis of fulfilment speed or time-to-ship calculated from these timestamps will produce negative values for 9.1% of records, silently distorting average shipping time figures. The TIME_TO_SHIP column added during cleaning flags these rows so downstream analysts know not to include them in fulfilment KPIs without first resolving the recording gap with the operations team.

Observation 2 — Missing marketing channel data suggests attribution gaps in digital campaigns

  • 83 orders (0.38% of records) have no marketing channel recorded. This is a small proportion, but in a business context it points to a gap in campaign attribution tracking rather than a data entry problem.
  • Marketing channel data is typically populated automatically from UTM parameters or referral source tracking at the point of purchase. When it is missing, it usually means a customer arrived through a channel that was not tagged — a direct visit, a forwarded link, or a paid placement without UTM parameters attached.
  • For a marketing analyst, unattributed orders represent spend that cannot be credited to any campaign. If these 83 orders represent a consistent pattern (e.g., concentrated in a specific product category or time period), it would indicate a specific campaign or channel that is not being tracked correctly. Replacing these blanks with "unknown" is the correct short-term fix, but the longer-term recommendation would be to audit UTM parameter coverage across all active campaigns.

Observation 3 — Duplicate order IDs at 0.66% of records are worth investigating before any sales reporting

  • 145 duplicate order IDs were identified. This is a small absolute number, but duplicates in an order ID field are significant because order IDs are typically designed to be unique primary keys. Their presence means the uniqueness constraint is either not enforced at the database level or is being bypassed somewhere in the order pipeline.
  • There are two plausible explanations. The first is legitimate: split shipments, where one order is fulfilled across multiple deliveries, may generate a second record under the same order ID. The second is a data quality issue: orders submitted twice (double-clicks, session errors, system retries) that were not caught by the order management system.
  • For a sales analyst, including duplicate order IDs without investigation risks double-counting revenue. Even at 0.66% of records, double-counted orders would inflate total revenue figures in any report built on this dataset. The 145 duplicates were documented and left for stakeholder review rather than deleted, because the correct treatment depends on which of the two explanations applies.
Assumptions and Caveats
  • The single missing purchase_ts value was left as-is. At 0.005% of records, imputing one timestamp without additional context would introduce more uncertainty than leaving it blank.
  • The 34 rows with $0 or missing usd_price were left as-is. These could represent refunded orders, promotional items, or data entry errors — only a stakeholder can confirm the correct treatment before any imputation.
  • The 27 rows with missing country_code were left as-is. Without any other geographic indicator in those rows, there was no basis for imputation.
  • The 2,000 rows where ship date preceded purchase date were flagged but not deleted. Removing 9.1% of the dataset without stakeholder confirmation would materially affect downstream analysis.
  • The 145 duplicate order_id records were documented and left for review. Duplicates may indicate legitimate split shipments or genuine errors — only a stakeholder can determine the correct interpretation.
© 2026 Richard Gamarra · Data Analyst← Back to portfolio
Independent SQLChart.jsRFM AnalysisCustomer Segmentation

Customer Segmentation
with RFM Analysis

This project uses RFM analysis to segment e-commerce customers by purchasing behavior so stakeholders can identify high-value customers, detect churn risk, and prioritize retention opportunities. I cleaned the Online Retail dataset in MySQL, created customer-level RFM scores, assigned business-friendly segments, and presented the results as a polished portfolio case study.

Tools UsedMySQL & Chart.js
Customers Analysed4,338
Total Revenue$8,887,227
DatasetUCI Online Retail II
Interactive Dashboard SQL Scripts
BackgroundData structureExecutive summaryDashboardInsightsRecommendationsMethodologySQL
Project Background

The business had limited visibility into customer purchasing behavior, making it difficult to know which customers were high value, which were at risk of churning, and which groups were worth targeting for retention.

This project applies RFM analysis to solve that problem. By scoring each customer on recency, frequency, and monetary value, the analysis creates practical customer groups such as Champions, Loyal Customers, Potential Loyalists, At Risk, Lost Customers, and Others. The goal is to turn raw transactions into segments that marketing and retention teams can act on immediately.

Data Structure & Initial Checks
online_retail_transactions_raw
InvoiceString
StockCodeString
DescriptionText
QuantityInt
InvoiceDateDatetime
PriceText → Decimal
Customer IDString
CountryString
rfm_scores (derived)
Customer IDString
RecencyInt
FrequencyInt
MonetaryDecimal
rfm_scores (scored + segmented)
Customer IDString
R_Score / F_Score / M_ScoreInt
SegmentString
Initial checks confirmed: The raw import contained 541,910 transaction-level rows. Because RFM is customer-level, 135,080 rows with missing Customer IDs were excluded. Negative quantities, zero or negative prices, and duplicate transaction lines were removed from the clean RFM table because they could distort recency, frequency, or monetary value.
Executive Summary
Overview of Findings

After cleaning and aggregating the transaction data, 4,338 customers were scored and assigned to six business-friendly RFM segments. The largest segment is At Risk with 1,308 customers, or 30.15% of the customer base, indicating that retention should be a priority.

Revenue is concentrated in Loyal Customers and Champions, who together generate about $6.67M in revenue. At Risk customers still represent over $1.61M in revenue, making them a valuable group to re-engage before they become lost.

Interactive RFM Dashboard
RFM Customer Segmentation Dashboard
Online Retail II · 4,338 customers · 6 segments · Visuals built directly in Chart.js
Customers segmented
4,338
customer profiles scored using RFM logic
Total analysed revenue
$8.89M
revenue represented in the final segmented table
Largest segment
1,308
At Risk customers, 30.15% of the base
Highest average spend
$5,171
Champions have the highest average value
01At Risk customers are the largest group at 1,308 customers and still represent $1.61M in revenue.
02Loyal Customers and Champions generate about $6.67M combined, making them the core revenue base.
03Champions have the highest average value at $5,170.63 per customer.
Customers by segment
The base is concentrated in At Risk and Loyal Customer groups, showing where retention and loyalty efforts should focus.
Revenue by segment
Revenue concentration shows which customer groups drive the most commercial value.
Average spend per customer
Champions and Loyal Customers spend far more per customer than lower-engagement groups.
Recency vs monetary value
Shows which segments combine recent activity with higher customer value.
Segment share overview
Share of customers by RFM segment shown as a bar chart for clearer comparison.
Why the dashboard was rebuilt directly in code. Instead of relying on a static Tableau screenshot, the final visuals on this page are rendered as interactive charts in the browser. This keeps the RFM project visually consistent with Challenge 4 and makes the dashboard feel like part of the portfolio itself rather than an external embed.
Insights Deep Dive

Each insight is grouped by business theme and paired with the visualization that supports it. The goal is not just to report what the segments look like, but to explain what the pattern means for the business and why it matters.

Insight 1 — The customer base is dominated by disengagement, not loyalty

  • At Risk is the single largest segment at 1,308 customers (30.15% of the base) — meaning nearly 1 in 3 customers has already started drifting away.
  • This is not a surprise finding. In most e-commerce datasets, the At Risk segment is large because acquiring customers is easier than retaining them. What makes this significant is the scale: At Risk is larger than Loyal Customers (1,116) and Champions (606) combined.
  • A customer base where the largest group is disengaging is not in a steady state — it is in slow decline unless a retention strategy is actively counteracting churn. The absence of a win-back programme would accelerate that decline as At Risk customers move into Lost.
  • Potential Loyalists (727) are an encouraging signal — they are recent and showing some engagement — but they are still smaller than At Risk, meaning the pipeline is not yet replacing customers as fast as they are drifting away.
Customer count by segment
At Risk is the dominant segment by volume, which is the most important strategic signal in the entire dataset.

Insight 2 — Revenue is being carried by a small group that is not being protected

  • Loyal Customers and Champions together generate $6.67M — 75% of total revenue — from just 39.7% of the customer base.
  • This is a classic revenue concentration pattern: a small number of high-engagement customers are subsidising the economics of a much larger, lower-value base. The business is more fragile than the headline customer count suggests, because losing even a fraction of Loyal Customers or Champions would have a disproportionate revenue impact.
  • At Risk customers still generate $1.61M. This is money that is actively at risk of leaving. At Risk customers have purchased before, which means the acquisition cost has already been spent. Losing them means losing that $1.61M without any offsetting reduction in acquisition spend.
  • Lost Customers generate only $213K across 428 customers, which shows how quickly monetary value degrades once a customer stops engaging. An At Risk customer today is likely to look like a Lost Customer within 6–12 months without intervention.
Total revenue by segment
Revenue is concentrated in Loyal Customers and Champions. The gap between these segments and the rest of the base shows how much the business depends on a relatively small group.

Insight 3 — Champions spend 12× more per customer than Potential Loyalists, but the gap is closeable

  • Champions average $5,170.63 per customer. Potential Loyalists average $429.55. That is a 12× difference in value from customers who are both relatively recent and engaged.
  • The difference is explained by frequency, not by a single large purchase. Champions have bought many times at consistent values. Potential Loyalists have bought recently but infrequently, which is why their frequency and monetary scores are lower even though their recency score qualifies them as engaged.
  • This matters because Potential Loyalists are the most actionable segment for growth. They are already in the habit of buying from this retailer — the goal is to deepen that habit before it fades. A targeted email sequence, a loyalty programme invitation, or a personalised repeat-purchase offer are all lower-cost interventions than acquiring a new customer from scratch.
  • At Risk customers average $1,235.65, which is three times more than Potential Loyalists. This reinforces that At Risk is not a low-value group to deprioritise — these are customers with a demonstrated purchase history who have simply gone quiet.
Average spend per customer by segment
The value gap between Champions and every other segment is driven by repeat purchase frequency. Potential Loyalists are the closest segment to bridging that gap with the right intervention.

Insight 4 — Potential Loyalists are the highest-leverage growth opportunity in the base

  • Potential Loyalists number 727 customers — 20% more customers than the Champion segment (606) — which means the pipeline for growing Champions already exists at scale.
  • Potential Loyalists score well on recency: they have purchased recently enough to still be reachable and receptive. What they lack is frequency. They have not yet established a repeat-purchase habit, which is the defining behaviour that separates this segment from Loyal Customers and Champions.
  • The strategic implication is specific: the intervention for this segment is not a discount (which trains customers to wait for promotions) but a reason to return. Product recommendations based on their purchase history, early access to new stock, or a loyalty tier invitation are all more effective at building the repeat-purchase habit than a price reduction.
  • If even 20% of Potential Loyalists convert to Loyal Customer behaviour, that adds roughly 145 customers to the high-value base — without spending a single pound on acquisition.
Customer share by segment
Potential Loyalists represent a larger pool than Champions. Converting a fraction of them into repeat buyers is a higher-return investment than acquiring new customers from scratch.

Insight 5 — Recency separates recoverable customers from lost ones

  • Champions average 52 days since their last purchase. At Risk customers average 210 days. Lost Customers average 295 days. That 243-day gap between Champions and Lost is the window in which a customer can still be recovered.
  • Recency is the most predictive dimension of future behaviour in RFM analysis. A customer who bought 52 days ago is almost certainly still aware of the brand and receptive to communication. A customer who last bought 295 days ago has likely shifted their spending elsewhere, and re-engagement becomes significantly more expensive.
  • At Risk customers sit at 210 days — past the point of easy retention but not yet at the point of no return. This is the most time-sensitive group in the dataset. Every month that passes without intervention moves At Risk customers closer to the Lost segment, where the $213K average revenue per group suggests most value has already been lost.
  • The scatter plot below makes this visible: Champions cluster in the top-left (recent, high value). Lost Customers cluster in the bottom-right (distant, low value). At Risk sits in the middle — still valuable, but moving in the wrong direction.
Recency vs average monetary value by segment
The scatter plot shows the direction of travel for each segment. At Risk customers are mid-journey between the high-value cluster and the low-value, high-recency cluster of Lost Customers.
Recommendations

These recommendations follow directly from the segment distribution and dashboard findings.

  • Launch a save campaign for At Risk customers. Focus first on customers with meaningful historical spend but declining activity using personalised offers, reminder emails, or limited-time incentives.
  • Protect Loyal Customers and Champions. These segments generate the strongest revenue contribution and should receive loyalty benefits, early access, or VIP perks to maintain high lifetime value.
  • Convert Potential Loyalists into repeat buyers. Use personalised recommendations, cross-sell campaigns, and follow-up offers to increase purchase frequency.
  • Monitor Lost Customers separately. Lost Customers have low recency and low revenue contribution, so reactivation spend should be lower priority than At Risk or Potential Loyalist campaigns.
Methodology

The analysis followed five steps, starting with data preparation and ending with the final portfolio dashboard.

1
Load and inspect the transaction data. The Online Retail file was loaded into MySQL and reviewed for customer coverage, transaction fields, and raw issues that could affect customer-level aggregation.
2
Clean and filter the analysis set. Rows without Customer ID were excluded, negative quantities were treated as returns/cancellations, zero or negative prices were removed, and duplicates were handled before calculating customer metrics.
3
Create customer-level RFM metrics. A derived table was built where recency measured days since the most recent purchase, frequency counted distinct invoices, and monetary value summed customer revenue.
4
Score and segment customers. NTILE(5) window functions were used to generate R, F, and M scores, and SQL CASE logic translated those scores into business-friendly customer segments.
5
Build the final interactive dashboard. The finished visuals were recreated directly in the portfolio using Chart.js so the project has interactive charts and a polished, stakeholder-facing layout.
SQL Cleaning Queries

This section shows the database setup used before running the RFM analysis.

Create table and load data
-- 01_create_tables.sql
-- This script creates the online_retail_transactions table for analysis.
-- It includes fields related to customer transactions from an online retail dataset.
-- Dataset source: UCI Machine Learning Repository - Online Retail II

USE online_retail_ii;

CREATE TABLE online_retail_transactions (
    InvoiceNo VARCHAR(20),
    StockCode VARCHAR(20),
    Description TEXT,
    Quantity INT,
    InvoiceDate DATETIME,
    UnitPrice DECIMAL(15, 4),
    CustomerID VARCHAR(20),
    Country VARCHAR(50)
);

-- Note: No primary key defined due to duplicate invoices or items in raw data.
-- Consider indexing on InvoiceDate or CustomerID for performance.

-- Loads retail transaction data from CSV into the online_retail_transactions table.
-- CSV must be placed in MySQL's secure Uploads folder.

LOAD DATA INFILE 'C:/ProgramData/MySQL/MySQL Server 8.0/Uploads/online_retail_final.csv'
INTO TABLE online_retail_transactions
FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
ENCLOSED BY '"'
LINES TERMINATED BY '
'
IGNORE 1 ROWS
(InvoiceNo, StockCode, Description, Quantity, InvoiceDate, UnitPrice, CustomerID, Country);
RFM Segmentation Queries
RFM scoring and segmentation
-- Create the RFM view (datetime-safe)
CREATE VIEW rfm_scores AS
SELECT
    CustomerID,
    DATEDIFF('2011-12-10', MAX(InvoiceDate)) AS Recency,
    COUNT(DISTINCT InvoiceNo) AS Frequency,
    ROUND(SUM(Quantity * UnitPrice), 2) AS Monetary
FROM online_retail_transactions
WHERE CustomerID IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY CustomerID;

-- Create the segmented table using NTILE
CREATE TABLE rfm_segmented_customers AS
SELECT *,
    NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY Recency ASC) AS R_Score,
    NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY Frequency DESC) AS F_Score,
    NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY Monetary DESC) AS M_Score
FROM rfm_scores;

-- Add and update the Segment column
ALTER TABLE rfm_segmented_customers
ADD COLUMN Segment VARCHAR(30);

UPDATE rfm_segmented_customers
SET Segment = CASE
    WHEN R_Score >= 4 AND F_Score >= 4 AND M_Score = 5 THEN 'Champions'
    WHEN R_Score >= 3 AND F_Score >= 4 AND M_Score >= 4 THEN 'Loyal Customers'
    WHEN M_Score = 5 AND F_Score <= 2 AND R_Score <= 3 THEN 'Big Spenders'
    WHEN R_Score >= 3 AND F_Score >= 2 AND M_Score >= 3 THEN 'Potential Loyalists'
    WHEN R_Score = 5 AND F_Score = 1 AND M_Score <= 3 THEN 'New Customers'
    WHEN R_Score <= 2 AND (F_Score >= 3 OR M_Score >= 3) THEN 'At Risk'
    WHEN R_Score <= 2 AND F_Score <= 2 AND M_Score <= 2 THEN 'Hibernating'
    WHEN R_Score = 1 AND F_Score = 1 AND M_Score = 1 THEN 'Lost'
    ELSE 'About to Sleep'
END;

SELECT Segment, COUNT(*) AS Customer_Count
FROM rfm_segmented_customers
GROUP BY Segment
ORDER BY Customer_Count DESC;

SELECT *
FROM rfm_segmented_customers;
Assumptions and Caveats
Caveats & Assumptions: The reference date was set to 2011-12-10 because it follows the final transaction date used in the dataset. The RFM scores are relative to this dataset only, so segment definitions should be interpreted within this customer base rather than as universal rules. Rows with missing Customer IDs, negative quantities, invalid prices, and duplicate transaction lines were excluded from the clean RFM table to protect the reliability of customer-level metrics. Segment labels were assigned using rule-based SQL logic and would need stakeholder review before being used in live campaigns.
© 2026 Richard Gamarra · Data Analyst← Back to portfolio
